# Security Review

You are an internal InfoSec team member conducting a security-focused code review.

## Your Perspective
- Assume breach mentality - what could go wrong?
- Think like an attacker examining this code
- Consider both immediate vulnerabilities and systemic risks

## Review Scope
Review these changes:

 2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
-3. After authorisation, copy the code from the browser's address bar
-4. Paste it back into the terminal
+3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
+4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)
+
+**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.
 
 ### 8. Verify

index f52d854..df3d68e 100644
--- a/google-workspace/SKILL.md
+++ b/google-workspace/SKILL.md
@@ -1,7 +1,7 @@
 ---
 name: Google Workspace
 description: Read-only Gmail and Contacts access with configurable Calendar (readonly or readwrite) for OpenClaw agents
-version: 0.1.0
+version: 0.2.0
 author: panthrocorp
 license: MIT-0
 metadata:
@@ -117,6 +117,8 @@ google-workspace auth status
 
 If the token has expired, ask the operator to re-authenticate by running `google-workspace auth login` on the host.
 
+If authentication fails with `Error 400: policy_enforced`, the operator's Google account likely has Advanced Protection enabled. They will need to temporarily unenroll, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists across sessions.
+
 ## Output format
 
 All commands output JSON by default. Use `--output text` for plain text where supported.

## Security Checklist

> Pre-commit gates in this estate (`checkov`, `trivy`, `gitleaks`, `detect-secrets`) cover many findings automatically. For each issue found, note whether it would be caught by existing tooling (and therefore represents a hook bypass or config gap) or is a genuine gap in coverage.

### OWASP Top 10
Relevant primarily for application code. For infrastructure/IaC code, focus on the items below and skip those that do not apply (XSS, XXE, deserialisation rarely apply to Terraform).
- [ ] Injection flaws (command injection in provisioners, shell scripts, user data)
- [ ] Broken authentication / credential exposure
- [ ] Sensitive data exposure (state files, outputs, logs)
- [ ] Broken access control (IAM over-permissioning, missing resource policies)
- [ ] Security misconfiguration (open security groups, public buckets, unencrypted storage)
- [ ] Using components with known vulnerabilities (pinned provider/module versions, CVEs)
- [ ] Insufficient logging and monitoring (CloudTrail, VPC flow logs, audit logging)

### Infrastructure as Code Security
- [ ] No secrets, tokens, or credentials in Terraform code, tfvars, or state
- [ ] Sensitive outputs marked `sensitive = true`
- [ ] IAM policies follow least privilege; no wildcards (`*`) on actions or resources without justification
- [ ] Critical resources protected with `prevent_destroy = true` in lifecycle block
- [ ] S3 buckets: public access blocked, versioning enabled, encryption at rest
- [ ] Checkov skip comments include an inline explanation (`#checkov:skip=CKV_ID:reason`)
- [ ] No `depends_on` used to work around missing IAM propagation without comment
- [ ] Provider credentials not hardcoded; auth is environment-driven or IRSA/OIDC

### Kubernetes & Infrastructure Security
- [ ] Security contexts (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, capabilities)
- [ ] Pod security standards compliance
- [ ] Network policies and segmentation
- [ ] RBAC - principle of least privilege
- [ ] Service account permissions
- [ ] Resource limits (prevent DoS)
- [ ] Image security (base images, vulnerability scanning)
- [ ] Privileged containers or host access

### Secrets & Credentials
- [ ] Hardcoded secrets, tokens, or passwords
- [ ] Secrets in environment variables vs mounted volumes
- [ ] External secrets management integration
- [ ] Credential rotation capabilities
- [ ] Secrets in logs or error messages

### Supply Chain Security
- [ ] Dependency versions pinned
- [ ] Image tags pinned (no :latest)
- [ ] Trusted registries only
- [ ] SBOM considerations
- [ ] Third-party component risks

### Data Protection
- [ ] Encryption at rest
- [ ] Encryption in transit (TLS)
- [ ] PII handling
- [ ] Data retention and disposal
- [ ] Backup security

## Output Format

For each finding, provide:

### [CRITICAL|HIGH|MEDIUM|LOW|INFO] - Brief Title

**Location**: file:line or resource reference
**Issue**: Clear description of the vulnerability
**Risk**: What could an attacker do with this?
**Recommendation**: Specific fix or mitigation
**References**: CVE, CWE, or documentation links if applicable

---

Begin your security review. Be thorough but prioritise by risk. If no issues found in a category, note it briefly. Challenge assumptions and question defaults.

+If authentication fails with `Error 400: policy_enforced`, the operator's Google account likely has Advanced Protection enabled. They will need to temporarily unenroll, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists across sessions.
+
 ## Output format
 
 All commands output JSON by default. Use `--output text` for plain text where supported.

## Deployment Quality Checklist

### Configuration Consistency
- [ ] Environment-specific values properly separated
- [ ] No hardcoded environment-specific values in templates
- [ ] Consistent naming conventions
- [ ] Values documented or self-explanatory
- [ ] Defaults are sensible for all environments

### Rollout Safety
- [ ] Rolling update strategy appropriate
- [ ] Health checks configured (liveness, readiness, startup probes)
- [ ] Probe timing sensible (not too aggressive)
- [ ] Resource requests/limits defined
- [ ] PodDisruptionBudgets where needed
- [ ] Graceful shutdown handling

### Failure Modes
- [ ] What happens when dependencies are unavailable?
- [ ] Retry logic and backoff strategies
- [ ] Circuit breaker patterns where appropriate
- [ ] Timeout configurations
- [ ] Error handling doesn't mask issues

### Observability
- [ ] Logging configured appropriately
- [ ] Metrics exposed
- [ ] Alerts defined for critical failures
- [ ] Dashboards or monitoring coverage
- [ ] Trace correlation if applicable

### Operational Concerns
- [ ] Scaling behaviour (HPA/VPA/KEDA)
- [ ] Resource sizing appropriate for workload
- [ ] Storage considerations (PVC sizing, retention)
- [ ] Backup and recovery procedures
- [ ] Runbook or operational documentation

### Upgrade & Maintenance
- [ ] Zero-downtime upgrade path exists
- [ ] Rollback procedure is clear (revert commit, re-apply, or manual steps documented)
- [ ] Version compatibility checked (provider upgrades, API deprecations)
- [ ] No destructive resource replacements triggered by the change without explicit intent

### Terraform-Specific Deployment Quality
- [ ] Change will produce a deterministic plan (no `known after apply` surprises that could block CI)
- [ ] Resources that will be destroyed and recreated are intentional; `prevent_destroy` considered for critical resources
- [ ] `ignore_changes` usage is justified and documented
- [ ] Provider and module version constraints do not allow unexpected upgrades mid-pipeline
- [ ] Workspace drift: does this change assume state that may not exist yet in all target workspaces?
- [ ] Run trigger dependencies are correct; upstream workspaces apply before this one

### Multi-Environment Validation
- [ ] Works in dev/stg/prod with appropriate differences
- [ ] Secrets/config sources correct per environment
- [ ] Network policies allow required traffic
- [ ] DNS and service discovery correct

## Output Format

For each finding, provide:

### [BLOCKER|MAJOR|MINOR|SUGGESTION] - Brief Title

**Location**: file:line or resource reference
**Issue**: What's wrong or missing
**Impact**: What breaks or degrades because of this
**Recommendation**: Specific fix
**Testing**: How to validate the fix

---

Begin your QA review. Focus on what could cause deployment failures, operational incidents, or silent degradation. Be practical - not everything needs to be perfect, but risks should be acknowledged.

## Review Focus

- Logic correctness
- Edge cases handled

content.text:
## Security Review

### MEDIUM - Advising Users to Disable Advanced Protection

**Location**: `google-workspace/README.md`
**Issue**: The documentation explicitly instructs users to temporarily unenroll from Google Advanced Protection to complete OAuth. Advanced Protection is Google's strongest account security tier, designed for high-risk users.
**Risk**: An operator following this guidance reduces their account security posture during a time when they are actively handling OAuth credentials. This creates a window of elevated exposure. It also normalises disabling security controls as a routine operational step.
**Recommendation**: Add a stronger warning that this is a significant security trade-off. Recommend the operator consult their security team before doing this. If this is a recurring requirement, the better fix is getting the OAuth app verified by Google, which removes the block entirely without requiring users to weaken their protection.
**References**: [Google Advanced Protection overview](https://landing.google.com/advancedprotection/)

**Tooling coverage**: Not caught by `gitleaks`, `detect-secrets`, or `checkov`. Genuine documentation gap.

---

### INFO - No secrets, credentials, or supply chain concerns

The version bump and documentation changes introduce no new code paths, no credentials, no hardcoded values, and no dependency changes. All other checklist items are N/A for this diff.

---

## QA Review

### SUGGESTION - "Persists on the EBS volume" is environment-specific

**Location**: `google-workspace/README.md`
**Issue**: The sentence "The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once" hardcodes an assumption about the deployment target (EBS). If the skill is deployed elsewhere (local, non-EBS block storage, etc.) the statement is misleading.
**Impact**: Operators on non-EBS deployments may be confused or incorrectly assume persistence when it may not apply.
**Recommendation**: Generalise to "The refresh token is stored on the host volume, so you only need to do this once per host" or similar.
**Testing**: N/A - doc change.

---

### MINOR - SKILL.md and README.md are inconsistent on Advanced Protection

**Location**: `google-workspace/SKILL.md:120` vs `google-workspace/README.md`
**Issue**: The README includes the unenrol/re-enrol instruction in full. SKILL.md only says "they will need to temporarily unenrol, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enrol" with no link or further detail. An agent using SKILL.md as context gets a less complete picture.
**Impact**: An AI agent working from SKILL.md alone cannot guide an operator through the full remediation.
**Recommendation**: Either add the link to `myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection` in SKILL.md too, or reference the README for full steps.
**Testing**: N/A - doc change.

---

All deployment, rollout, and Terraform checklist items are N/A for this diff.

---

## Peer Review

### Overall Assessment

**Approve with comments.** The OAuth flow clarification is genuinely useful and fixes a real UX problem. The Advanced Protection guidance solves an immediate pain point. Two things worth addressing before merge.

### The Good

The code extraction clarification (`code=` to `&scope=`) is exactly the right level of detail. Anyone who has hit that confusion will immediately understand it. Good fix.

### Concerns

`README.md` - "Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection" is operationally correct advice but it's presented too casually. This is not a routine config tweak. Advanced Protection is specifically for people under elevated threat. A one-liner instruction to disable it, followed by "then re-enrol", understates the risk significantly. At minimum add a note that this should be treated as a deliberate security decision, not a routine step. The better longer-term fix is getting the app verified.

`README.md` - "The refresh token persists on the EBS volume" - EBS is too specific. This skill can run on other storage backends. Rephrase to refer to the host volume or credential directory (`~/.openclaw/credentials/`) instead.

### Suggestions

`SKILL.md` - The new troubleshooting line and the README entry are slightly inconsistent in detail. Not blocking, but worth aligning so agents using SKILL.md get the same guidance as human operators reading the README.

### Questions

Is the version bump to 0.2.0 intentional for what is essentially a documentation change? Clawhub semver conventions would typically treat this as a patch (0.1.1). Worth confirming whether clawhub treats doc updates as minor bumps.

---

**Summary across all three reviews:** The main flag is the Advanced Protection unenrol advice being presented without adequate risk framing. The EBS-specificity in the README is a minor cleanup. The version bump is worth a sanity check. No security or deployment blockers.

1	# google-workspace
2	
3	![Language](https://img.shields.io/badge/language-Go-00ADD8?logo=go&logoColor=white)
4	![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT--0-green)
5	![Platform](https://img.shields.io/badge/platform-linux%2Farm64-lightgrey)
6	
7	A custom OpenClaw skill providing read-only Gmail, configurable Calendar, and read-only Contacts access via the Google APIs. Built for environments where the agent instance is treated as potentially hostile.
8	
9	## Why not use an existing skill?
10	
11	Every existing Google-related skill on clawskills.sh requests broad read-write OAuth scopes and most are flagged suspicious by VirusTotal or OpenClaw moderation. This skill enforces strict scope boundaries at three levels: code (no write functions for Gmail/Contacts), config (Calendar write is opt-in), and Google Cloud project (only required APIs enabled).
12	
13	## Services
14	
15	| Service | OAuth Scope | Mode | Write code paths |
16	|---------|------------|------|-----------------|
17	| Gmail | `gmail.readonly` | Read-only | None |
18	| Calendar | `calendar.readonly` or `calendar.events` | Configurable: `off`, `readonly`, `readwrite` | Gated by config check |
19	| Contacts | `contacts.readonly` | Read-only | None |
20	
21	## Installation
22	
23	```bash
24	clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
25	```
26	
27	Or build from source:
28	
29	```bash
30	GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o google-workspace .
31	```
32	
33	## Deployment guide
34	
35	Follow these steps in order to deploy the skill to an OpenClaw instance.
36	
37	### 1. Create a Google Cloud project
38	
39	1. Create a new project in the [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/)
40	2. Enable the following APIs only:
41	   - Gmail API
42	   - Google Calendar API
43	   - People API (Contacts)
44	3. Configure the OAuth consent screen (External, but only used by the operator's own account)
45	4. Create an OAuth 2.0 client ID with application type **Desktop**
46	5. Note the **Client ID** and **Client Secret**
47	
48	### 2. Create secrets in Bitwarden Secrets Manager
49	
50	Create three secrets in Bitwarden SM (EU region, PanthroCorp project):
51	
52	| Secret key | Value |
53	|------------|-------|
54	| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Random 64-character hex string (`openssl rand -hex 32`) |
55	| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client ID from step 1 |
56	| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>` | Client secret from step 1 |
57	
58	### 3. Add Terraform configuration
59	
60	In the `openclaw` repo, add the Bitwarden secret key references to the instance's tfvars file:
61	
62	```hcl
63	bitwarden_secret_key_google_workspace_token_key = "GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
64	bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_id           = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
65	bitwarden_secret_key_google_client_secret       = "GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET_OPENCLAW_AWS_SANDBOX_<INSTANCE>"  # pragma: allowlist secret
66	```
67	
68	### 4. Deploy
69	
70	Merge the Terraform changes to `main`. The deploy workflow will:
71	- Create SSM parameters for the three secrets
72	- Update the `.env` file on the instance with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
73	- Create the `config/credentials/google-workspace/` directory
74	
75	### 5. Install the skill on the instance
76	
77	SSH in via Twingate and install:
78	
79	```bash
80	ssh -i ~/.ssh/openclaw-operator ubuntu@<domain>
81	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
82	```
83	
84	### 6. Configure scopes
85	
86	```bash
87	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
88	  google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
89	```
90	
91	### 7. Authenticate with Google
92	
93	```bash
94	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
95	  google-workspace auth login
96	```
97	
98	1. Copy the URL printed to the terminal
99	2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
100	3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
101	4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)
102	
103	**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.
104	
105	### 8. Verify
106	
107	```bash
108	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace auth status
109	sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace gmail labels
110	```
111	
112	No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.
113	
114	## Prerequisites
115	
116	- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled (see deployment guide above)
117	- An OAuth 2.0 "Desktop" client configured in that project
118	- Three environment variables on the host:
119	  - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` from the OAuth client
120	  - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` for encrypting the stored OAuth token (random 64-char hex string)
121	
122	## Usage
123	
124	### Configure scopes
125	
126	```bash
127	google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
128	google-workspace config show
129	```
130	
131	### Authenticate
132	
133	```bash
134	google-workspace auth login
135	```
136	
137	This prints a URL. Open it in your browser, authorise the requested scopes, then paste the authorisation code back into the terminal.
138	
139	### Gmail (read-only)
140	
141	```bash
142	google-workspace gmail search --query "from:boss@example.com" --max-results 5
143	google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
144	google-workspace gmail labels
145	google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
146	```
147	
148	### Calendar
149	
150	```bash
151	google-workspace calendar list
152	google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-07T23:59:59Z
153	google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID
154	
155	# Only available in readwrite mode:
156	google-workspace calendar create --summary "Standup" --start "2026-04-01T09:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T09:15:00Z"
157	google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
158	google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
159	```
160	
161	### Contacts (read-only)
162	
163	```bash
164	google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
165	google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
166	google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
167	```
168	
169	### Check auth status
170	
171	```bash
172	google-workspace auth status
173	```
174	
175	## Token storage
176	
177	OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key derived via HKDF-SHA256. The encryption key comes from the `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` environment variable, which should be injected from an external secret manager (e.g. Bitwarden SM, AWS SSM Parameter Store). The binary refuses to store tokens if this variable is not set.
178	
179	Default token location: `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/token.enc`
180	
181	## Security
182	
183	- Gmail has no send, modify, or delete code paths. The `internal/google/gmail.go` file only contains `messages.list`, `messages.get`, `labels.list`, and `threads` operations.
184	- Contacts has no create, update, or delete code paths.
185	- Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error if the mode is `readonly`.
186	- The Google Cloud project should only have Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled, providing server-side scope enforcement.
187	- Token encryption uses a random salt per encryption, preventing identical tokens from producing identical ciphertext.
188	
189	## Development
190	
191	```bash
192	go build -o google-workspace .
193	go test ./...
194	go vet ./...
195	```
196

198

Every existing Google-related skill on clawskills.sh requests broad read-write OAuth scopes and most are flagged suspicious by VirusTotal or OpenClaw moderation. ## Services


Or build from source:

```bash
GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o google-workspace .
```

## Deployment guide

Follow these steps in order to deploy the skill to an OpenClaw instance.

### 1. Create a Google Cloud project

2. Enable the following APIs only:
   - Gmail API
   - Google Calendar API
   - People API (Contacts)

- Update the `.env` file on the instance with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`
- Create the `config/credentials/google-workspace/` directory

### 5. Install the skill on the instance

SSH in via Twingate and install:

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/openclaw-operator ubuntu@<domain>
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway clawhub install panthrocorp-google-workspace
```

### 6. Configure scopes

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
```

### 7. Authenticate with Google

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway \
  google-workspace auth login
```

1. Copy the URL printed to the terminal
2. Open it in your local browser and authenticate with your Google account
3. After authorisation, the browser will redirect to `http://localhost` (which will fail to load). Copy only the `code` parameter value from the URL bar (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=`)
4. Paste only the code back into the terminal (not the full URL)

**Google Advanced Protection users:** If you see `Error 400: policy_enforced`, Advanced Protection blocks unverified third-party OAuth apps. Temporarily unenroll from Advanced Protection at `https://myaccount.google.com/advanced-protection`, complete the OAuth flow, then re-enroll. The refresh token persists on the EBS volume so you only need to do this once.

### 8. Verify

```bash
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace auth status
sudo -u openclaw docker exec -it openclaw-gateway google-workspace gmail labels
```

No container restart is needed. The token is persisted on the EBS volume and the binary reads it fresh on each invocation.

## Prerequisites

- A Google Cloud project with Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled (see deployment guide above)
- An OAuth 2.0 "Desktop" client configured in that project
- Three environment variables on the host:
  - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` from the OAuth client
  - `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` for encrypting the stored OAuth token (random 64-char hex string)

## Usage

### Configure scopes

```bash
google-workspace config set --gmail=true --calendar=readonly --contacts=true
google-workspace config show
```

### Authenticate

```bash
google-workspace auth login
```

This prints a URL. Open it in your browser, authorise the requested scopes, then paste the authorisation code back into the terminal.

### Gmail (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace gmail search --query "from:boss@example.com" --max-results 5
google-workspace gmail read --id MESSAGE_ID
google-workspace gmail labels
google-workspace gmail threads --query "subject:meeting"
```

### Calendar

```bash
google-workspace calendar list
google-workspace calendar events --from 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --to 2026-04-07T23:59:59Z
google-workspace calendar event --id EVENT_ID

# Only available in readwrite mode:
google-workspace calendar create --summary "Standup" --start "2026-04-01T09:00:00Z" --end "2026-04-01T09:15:00Z"
google-workspace calendar update --id EVENT_ID --summary "Updated title"
google-workspace calendar delete --id EVENT_ID
```

### Contacts (read-only)

```bash
google-workspace contacts list --max-results 50
google-workspace contacts search --query "John"
google-workspace contacts get --id "people/c1234567890"
```

### Check auth status

```bash
google-workspace auth status
```

## Token storage

OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key derived via HKDF-SHA256. The encryption key comes from the `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` environment variable, which should be injected from an external secret manager (e.g. Bitwarden SM, AWS SSM Parameter Store). The binary refuses to store tokens if this variable is not set.

Default token location: `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/token.enc`

## Security

- Gmail has no send, modify, or delete code paths. The `internal/google/gmail.go` file only contains `messages.list`, `messages.get`, `labels.list`, and `threads` operations.
- Contacts has no create, update, or delete code paths.
- Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error if the mode is `readonly`.
- The Google Cloud project should only have Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API enabled, providing server-side scope enforcement.
- Token encryption uses a random salt per encryption, preventing identical tokens from producing identical ciphertext.

## Development

```bash
go build -o google-workspace .
go test ./...
go vet ./...
```

25		}
26

28		cSecret := clientSecret()
29		if cID == "" || cSecret == "" {

31		}

44		}
45	
46		oauthCfg := oauth.NewOAuthConfig(cID, cSecret)
47		ts := oauthCfg.TokenSource(ctx, token)
48	
49		// Wrap the token source so that a refreshed token is persisted back to disk.
50		pts := &persistingTokenSource{inner: ts, dir: configDir, key: key}

56	// persistingTokenSource wraps an oauth2.TokenSource and saves refreshed tokens to disk.
57	type persistingTokenSource struct {
58		inner oauth2.TokenSource
59		dir   string
60		key   string
61		last  *oauth2.Token
62	}
63	
64	func (p *persistingTokenSource) Token() (*oauth2.Token, error) {
65		t, err := p.inner.Token()
66		if err != nil {
67			return nil, err
68		}
69		// Save if the token changed (i.e. was refreshed).
70		if p.last == nil || t.AccessToken != p.last.AccessToken {
71			_ = oauth.SaveToken(p.dir, t, p.key) // best-effort; don't fail the request
72			p.last = t
73		}

1	package oauth
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"fmt"
6		"net/url"
7	
8		"golang.org/x/oauth2"
9	)
10	
11	const (
12		authURL  = "https://accounts.zoho.eu/oauth/v2/auth"
13		tokenURL = "https://accounts.zoho.eu/oauth/v2/token"
14	
15		// RedirectURL must be registered in the Zoho API Console.
16		RedirectURL = "http://localhost:8080/callback"
17	)
18	
19	// Scopes required for full mail access.
20	var Scopes = []string{
21		"ZohoMail.messages.ALL",
22		"ZohoMail.folders.ALL",
23		"ZohoMail.accounts.READ",
24	}
25	
26	var zohoEndpoint = oauth2.Endpoint{
27		AuthURL:  authURL,
28		TokenURL: tokenURL,
29	}
30	
31	// NewOAuthConfig builds an OAuth2 config for the Zoho Mail API.
32	func NewOAuthConfig(clientID, clientSecret string) *oauth2.Config {
33		return &oauth2.Config{
34			ClientID:     clientID,
35			ClientSecret: clientSecret,
36			Endpoint:     zohoEndpoint,
37			RedirectURL:  RedirectURL,
38			Scopes:       Scopes,
39		}
40	}
41	
42	// InteractiveLogin performs the OAuth2 authorisation code flow for a headless server.
43	// It prints a URL for the operator to visit, then reads the full redirect URL from stdin
44	// and extracts the authorisation code.
45	func InteractiveLogin(ctx context.Context, cfg *oauth2.Config) (*oauth2.Token, error) {
46		authURL := cfg.AuthCodeURL("state", oauth2.AccessTypeOffline)
47	
48		fmt.Println("Visit this URL in your browser to authorise access:")
49		fmt.Println()
50		fmt.Println("  " + authURL)
51		fmt.Println()
52		fmt.Printf("After authorising, you will be redirected to %s\n", RedirectURL)
53		fmt.Println("Paste the full redirect URL here: ")
54	
55		var rawURL string
56		if _, err := fmt.Scan(&rawURL); err != nil {
57			return nil, fmt.Errorf("reading redirect URL: %w", err)
58		}
59	
60		u, err := url.Parse(rawURL)
61		if err != nil {
62			return nil, fmt.Errorf("parsing redirect URL: %w", err)
63		}
64	
65		code := u.Query().Get("code")
66		if code == "" {
67			return nil, fmt.Errorf("no 'code' parameter found in redirect URL %q", rawURL)

# zoho-mail

## Overview

Full read/write Zoho Mail skill for OpenClaw agents. Unlike the google-workspace skill, this has no read-only restriction: it can list, read, send, reply, delete, and mark messages.

## Architecture

- `cmd/` — Cobra CLI. Subcommand groups `mail`, `folders`, `auth`, and `config` each register on `rootCmd`. `zohoClient()` in `cmd/mail.go` centralises config loading, token decryption, and API client construction.
- `internal/zoho/client.go` — Thin HTTP wrapper over the Zoho Mail REST API (`https://mail.zoho.eu/api`). Account ID is discovered on first API call via `GET /accounts` and cached in `Client.accountID`. All message and folder operations path-encode this ID.
- `internal/oauth/oauth.go` — OAuth2 flow with a localhost redirect (`http://localhost:8080/callback`). `InteractiveLogin` asks the operator to paste the **full redirect URL** back into the terminal (e.g. `http://localhost:8080/callback?code=...`). The code is then extracted from the URL. This differs from google-workspace, which asks for the code value only.
- `internal/crypto/` — AES-256-GCM with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation. Wire format: `salt (16B) || nonce (12B) || ciphertext+tag`. Same pattern as google-workspace.
- `persistingTokenSource` in `cmd/mail.go` — wraps the OAuth2 token source and saves a refreshed token back to disk whenever the access token changes. This keeps the stored token current without manual re-authentication.

## Key differences from google-workspace

- Full write access. No service modes and no config-level gating.
- OAuth redirect URL is `http://localhost:8080/callback`. The operator pastes the **full redirect URL** (not just the code).
- EU data centre only: all API and auth endpoints use `zoho.eu` domains.
- Config is minimal: only `email` field (`config.json`). No scope selection logic.

## Required environment variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `ZOHO_MAIL_TOKEN_KEY` | Passphrase for AES-256-GCM token encryption |
| `ZOHO_CLIENT_ID` | Zoho API Console client ID |
| `ZOHO_CLIENT_SECRET` | Zoho API Console client secret |

Config directory defaults to `~/.openclaw/credentials/zoho-mail/`. Override with `ZOHO_MAIL_CONFIG_DIR` or `--config-dir`.

## OAuth scopes

`ZohoMail.messages.ALL`, `ZohoMail.folders.ALL`, `ZohoMail.accounts.READ` — defined in `internal/oauth/oauth.go`.

## Missing before first release

- `.goreleaser.yml` — cross-compilation config required for clawhub publish
- `LICENSE` — MIT-0 required by clawhub
- Entry in `.release-please-config.json` and `.release-please-manifest.json`
- Entry in `.github/dependabot.yml`
- `go.sum` needs verification before tagging

# google-workspace

## Overview

Read-only Gmail and Contacts skill with configurable Calendar access. Security-first design: write code paths for Gmail and Contacts do not exist in the codebase.

## Architecture

- `cmd/` — Cobra CLI. Service commands (`gmail`, `calendar`, `contacts`, `auth`, `config`) register on `rootCmd`. Helper functions `gmailClient()`, `calendarClient()`, `contactsClient()` handle config loading, scope validation, token decryption, and API client construction inline.
- `internal/config/config.go` — JSON config model. `CalendarMode` is the only service-level gate (`off`, `readonly`, `readwrite`). `Config.OAuthScopes()` derives the OAuth scope list from the current config; scopes expand as modes are enabled. Changing the mode requires re-authentication to issue a new token with the updated scopes.
- `internal/oauth/` — OAuth2 Desktop flow with a localhost redirect. `InteractiveLogin` prompts for the **code value only** (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=` in the redirect URL). The browser fails to load `http://localhost`, and the operator copies the code parameter value from the URL bar. This differs from zoho-mail, which takes the full redirect URL.
- `internal/google/` — Thin typed wrappers around Google API services. Each wrapper exposes only the operations the skill permits: `GmailClient` has no send/modify/delete; `ContactsClient` has no write operations.
- `internal/crypto/` — AES-256-GCM with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation. Wire format: `salt (16B) || nonce (12B) || ciphertext+tag`.

## Scope enforcement (three layers)

1. Code: write methods do not exist in `internal/google/`.
2. Config: Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error otherwise.
3. Google Cloud project: only Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API should be enabled, providing server-side enforcement.

## Required environment variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` | Passphrase for AES-256-GCM token encryption |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` | Google OAuth2 client ID |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` | Google OAuth2 client secret |

Config directory defaults to `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/`. Override with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_CONFIG_DIR` or `--config-dir`.

## OAuth flow note

`InteractiveLogin` expects only the `code` value from the redirect URL, not the full URL. After the browser fails to load `http://localhost`, the operator copies the value of the `code=` parameter only and pastes it into the terminal. See `internal/oauth/oauth.go` for the implementation.

## Calendar mode and scope changes

If `CalendarMode` changes (e.g. `readonly` to `readwrite`), the stored token was issued with the old scopes. The operator must run `google-workspace auth login` again to issue a new token with the expanded scopes. The binary does not detect scope mismatches at runtime.

toolUseResult.content:
# google-workspace

## Overview

Read-only Gmail and Contacts skill with configurable Calendar access. Security-first design: write code paths for Gmail and Contacts do not exist in the codebase.

## Architecture

- `cmd/` — Cobra CLI. Service commands (`gmail`, `calendar`, `contacts`, `auth`, `config`) register on `rootCmd`. Helper functions `gmailClient()`, `calendarClient()`, `contactsClient()` handle config loading, scope validation, token decryption, and API client construction inline.
- `internal/config/config.go` — JSON config model. `CalendarMode` is the only service-level gate (`off`, `readonly`, `readwrite`). `Config.OAuthScopes()` derives the OAuth scope list from the current config; scopes expand as modes are enabled. Changing the mode requires re-authentication to issue a new token with the updated scopes.
- `internal/oauth/` — OAuth2 Desktop flow with a localhost redirect. `InteractiveLogin` prompts for the **code value only** (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=` in the redirect URL). The browser fails to load `http://localhost`, and the operator copies the code parameter value from the URL bar. This differs from zoho-mail, which takes the full redirect URL.
- `internal/google/` — Thin typed wrappers around Google API services. Each wrapper exposes only the operations the skill permits: `GmailClient` has no send/modify/delete; `ContactsClient` has no write operations.
- `internal/crypto/` — AES-256-GCM with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation. Wire format: `salt (16B) || nonce (12B) || ciphertext+tag`.

## Scope enforcement (three layers)

1. Code: write methods do not exist in `internal/google/`.
2. Config: Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error otherwise.
3. Google Cloud project: only Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API should be enabled, providing server-side enforcement.

## Required environment variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` | Passphrase for AES-256-GCM token encryption |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` | Google OAuth2 client ID |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` | Google OAuth2 client secret |

Config directory defaults to `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/`. Override with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_CONFIG_DIR` or `--config-dir`.

## OAuth flow note

`InteractiveLogin` expects only the `code` value from the redirect URL, not the full URL. After the browser fails to load `http://localhost`, the operator copies the value of the `code=` parameter only and pastes it into the terminal. See `internal/oauth/oauth.go` for the implementation.

## Calendar mode and scope changes

If `CalendarMode` changes (e.g. `readonly` to `readwrite`), the stored token was issued with the old scopes. The operator must run `google-workspace auth login` again to issue a new token with the expanded scopes. The binary does not detect scope mismatches at runtime.

# zoho-mail

## Overview

Full read/write Zoho Mail skill for OpenClaw agents. Unlike the google-workspace skill, this has no read-only restriction: it can list, read, send, reply, delete, and mark messages.

## Architecture

- `cmd/` — Cobra CLI. Subcommand groups `mail`, `folders`, `auth`, and `config` each register on `rootCmd`. `zohoClient()` in `cmd/mail.go` centralises config loading, token decryption, and API client construction.
- `internal/zoho/client.go` — Thin HTTP wrapper over the Zoho Mail REST API (`https://mail.zoho.eu/api`). Account ID is discovered on first API call via `GET /accounts` and cached in `Client.accountID`. All message and folder operations path-encode this ID.
- `internal/oauth/oauth.go` — OAuth2 flow with a localhost redirect (`http://localhost:8080/callback`). `InteractiveLogin` asks the operator to paste the **full redirect URL** back into the terminal (e.g. `http://localhost:8080/callback?code=...`). The code is then extracted from the URL. This differs from google-workspace, which asks for the code value only.
- `internal/crypto/` — AES-256-GCM with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation. Wire format: `salt (16B) || nonce (12B) || ciphertext+tag`. Same pattern as google-workspace.
- `persistingTokenSource` in `cmd/mail.go` — wraps the OAuth2 token source and saves a refreshed token back to disk whenever the access token changes. This keeps the stored token current without manual re-authentication.

## Key differences from google-workspace

- Full write access. No service modes and no config-level gating.
- OAuth redirect URL is `http://localhost:8080/callback`. The operator pastes the **full redirect URL** (not just the code).
- EU data centre only: all API and auth endpoints use `zoho.eu` domains.
- Config is minimal: only `email` field (`config.json`). No scope selection logic.

## Required environment variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `ZOHO_MAIL_TOKEN_KEY` | Passphrase for AES-256-GCM token encryption |
| `ZOHO_CLIENT_ID` | Zoho API Console client ID |
| `ZOHO_CLIENT_SECRET` | Zoho API Console client secret |

Config directory defaults to `~/.openclaw/credentials/zoho-mail/`. Override with `ZOHO_MAIL_CONFIG_DIR` or `--config-dir`.

## OAuth scopes

`ZohoMail.messages.ALL`, `ZohoMail.folders.ALL`, `ZohoMail.accounts.READ` — defined in `internal/oauth/oauth.go`.

# google-workspace

## Overview

Read-only Gmail and Contacts skill with configurable Calendar access. Security-first design: write code paths for Gmail and Contacts do not exist in the codebase.

## Architecture

- `cmd/` — Cobra CLI. Service commands (`gmail`, `calendar`, `contacts`, `auth`, `config`) register on `rootCmd`. Helper functions `gmailClient()`, `calendarClient()`, `contactsClient()` handle config loading, scope validation, token decryption, and API client construction inline.
- `internal/config/config.go` — JSON config model. `CalendarMode` is the only service-level gate (`off`, `readonly`, `readwrite`). `Config.OAuthScopes()` derives the OAuth scope list from the current config; scopes expand as modes are enabled. Changing the mode requires re-authentication to issue a new token with the updated scopes.
- `internal/oauth/` — OAuth2 Desktop flow with a localhost redirect. `InteractiveLogin` prompts for the **code value only** (the part after `code=` and before `&scope=` in the redirect URL). The browser fails to load `http://localhost`, and the operator copies the code parameter value from the URL bar. This differs from zoho-mail, which takes the full redirect URL.
- `internal/google/` — Thin typed wrappers around Google API services. Each wrapper exposes only the operations the skill permits: `GmailClient` has no send/modify/delete; `ContactsClient` has no write operations.
- `internal/crypto/` — AES-256-GCM with HKDF-SHA256 key derivation. Wire format: `salt (16B) || nonce (12B) || ciphertext+tag`.

## Scope enforcement (three layers)

1. Code: write methods do not exist in `internal/google/`.
2. Config: Calendar write operations check `config.CalendarMode == "readwrite"` at runtime and return an error otherwise.
3. Google Cloud project: only Gmail API, Calendar API, and People API should be enabled, providing server-side enforcement.

## Required environment variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_TOKEN_KEY` | Passphrase for AES-256-GCM token encryption |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` | Google OAuth2 client ID |
| `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` | Google OAuth2 client secret |

Config directory defaults to `~/.openclaw/credentials/google-workspace/`. Override with `GOOGLE_WORKSPACE_CONFIG_DIR` or `--config-dir`.

## OAuth flow note

`InteractiveLogin` expects only the `code` value from the redirect URL, not the full URL. After the browser fails to load `http://localhost`, the operator copies the value of the `code=` parameter only and pastes it into the terminal. See `internal/oauth/oauth.go` for the implementation.

## Calendar mode and scope changes

If `CalendarMode` changes (e.g. `readonly` to `readwrite`), the stored token was issued with the old scopes. The operator must run `google-workspace auth login` again to issue a new token with the expanded scopes. The binary does not detect scope mismatches at runtime.

1	package oauth
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"fmt"
6	
7		"golang.org/x/oauth2"
8		"golang.org/x/oauth2/google"
9	)
10	
11	// NewOAuthConfig builds an OAuth2 config for Google APIs using
12	// the Desktop app flow (redirect to localhost).
13	func NewOAuthConfig(clientID, clientSecret string, scopes []string) *oauth2.Config {
14		return &oauth2.Config{
15			ClientID:     clientID,
16			ClientSecret: clientSecret,
17			Endpoint:     google.Endpoint,
18			RedirectURL:  "http://localhost",
19			Scopes:       scopes,
20		}
21	}
22	
23	// InteractiveLogin performs the OAuth2 authorisation code flow for a headless
24	// server. It prints a URL for the user to visit, then reads the auth code
25	// from stdin.
26	func InteractiveLogin(ctx context.Context, cfg *oauth2.Config) (*oauth2.Token, error) {
27		url := cfg.AuthCodeURL("state", oauth2.AccessTypeOffline, oauth2.ApprovalForce)
28	
29		fmt.Println("Visit this URL in your browser to authorise access:")
30		fmt.Println()
31		fmt.Println("  " + url)
32		fmt.Println()
33		fmt.Print("Paste the authorisation code here: ")
34	
35		var code string
36		if _, err := fmt.Scan(&code); err != nil {
37			return nil, fmt.Errorf("reading auth code: %w", err)
38		}
39	
40		token, err := cfg.Exchange(ctx, code)
41		if err != nil {
42			return nil, fmt.Errorf("exchanging auth code: %w", err)
43		}
44	
45		return token, nil
46	}

# gh-ship: Branch → Commit → PR → CI → Merge → CI

> **GitHub repos only.** Before doing anything, confirm the repo has a GitHub remote (`git remote -v`).
> If the remote is Azure DevOps or anything other than `github.com`, stop and tell the user this command does not apply — use the ADO MCP tools instead.

Ship the current working changes end-to-end on GitHub. Run each phase in order, stopping if anything fails.

## Phase 1: Branch

Check current branch with `git branch --show-current`.

- If on `main` or `master`: create a feature branch. Derive the name from the staged changes or `ensuring that our skills are published to clawhub` if provided. Use kebab-case, prefixed with `fix/` or `feat/` per commit convention. Ask if unclear.
- If already on a feature branch: continue.

## Phase 2: Commit

Run `git status` and `git diff --cached` to understand what is staged.

- If nothing is staged: run `git diff --stat` to show unstaged changes, then ask which files to stage.
- Stage and commit following the project commit convention (semantic: `fix:`, `feat:`, `breaking:`; subject ≤50 chars; bullet body with emojis). Do not skip pre-commit hooks (`--no-verify` is forbidden unless the user explicitly requests it).
- If pre-commit hooks modify files, re-stage and commit again.

## Phase 3: Push & PR

Push the branch: `git push -u origin <branch>`.

Create the PR using `gh pr create`. Follow the project PR format:
- Short title (≤70 chars)
- Body with `## Summary` bullets and no test plan section
- Add `🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/claude-code)` footer

Show the PR URL.

## Phase 4: CI Check (post-PR)

Wait for CI to complete: `gh run list --branch <branch> --limit 5` then watch the most recent run with `gh run watch <run-id>`.

- If CI passes: proceed to Phase 5.
- If CI fails: show the failed job logs (`gh run view <run-id> --log-failed`), diagnose, fix, commit, push. Re-run Phase 4.

## Phase 5: Merge

Once CI is green: `gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch` (use squash unless the user specifies otherwise).

Confirm the merge completed and the branch was deleted.

## Phase 6: CI Check (post-merge)

Run `gh run list --branch main --limit 3` and watch the post-merge run.

- If CI passes: done. Report the merged PR URL and final status.
- If CI fails: report the failure with logs. Do not attempt auto-fix on main — flag it for the user.

## Notes

- `ensuring that our skills are published to clawhub` can provide a branch name, PR title hint, or both.
- Never force-push, never skip hooks, never push directly to main/master.
- If at any point the user needs to take a manual action, stop and clearly state what is needed.
